subject: Gaming
title: The $70 Game Debate
description: A quick, practical guide to The $70 Game Debate.

hook.text: Games cost $70 now. Players complain. But adjusted for inflation, games haven't actually gotten more expensive.

buildup.text: SNES games cost $60 in 1991. That's over $130 in today's money. Modern $70 games are historically cheap.

discovery.text: But development costs exploded. AAA games now cost $200-300 million to make. The math barely works.

twist.text: DLC, season passes, and microtransactions fill the gap. The $70 price is just the entry fee now.

climax.text: Gaming's pricing model is broken. Players pay more total while feeling each purchase is too expensive.

punchline.text: Seventy dollars feels expensive. But in 1991, you paid even more. Perspective shifts.

quiz.question: How do modern game prices compare to 1991 SNES games when adjusted for inflation?
options: SNES games at $60 would be over $130 today, making modern games relatively cheaper
Modern games are much more expensive
Prices have stayed exactly the same
